title: Connect Hierarchy, Rhythm, Type, and Feedback
summary: Connect Hierarchy, Rhythm, Type, and Feedback retrieves prior skills in an original community survey interface design critique scenario and requires implementation, behavior, failure-state, and transfer evidence.
objectives: Apply design information hierarchy, design spacing alignment, design color typography, design consistency feedback in unfamiliar constraints.
Produce or audit a real community survey interface design critique artifact without happy-path-only reasoning.
Preserve earlier requirements while testing keyboard, narrow-width, invalid, and assistive-semantic states.

steps.title: 04 · Read the community survey interface design critique contract · theory
steps.instruction: Checkpoint 4: Which practice survives changed data and devices? Scenario: community survey interface design critique.
steps.why: Connect Hierarchy, Rhythm, Type, and Feedback makes the decision observable and testable. This is checkpoint 4 in community survey interface design critique.

content.text: Hierarchy comes from content order, heading structure, grouping, scale, weight, contrast, and whitespace working together. Primary and secondary tasks need clear distinction without making every item loud. Visual order must agree with reading and focus order so emphasis does not create a different task sequence for different users. Proximity signals relationship. A small deliberate spacing scale, shared alignment edges, logical properties, and consistent group rhythm reduce visual parsing work. Whitespace is functional separation, not unused capacity. Dense or arbitrary gaps can make labels, help, errors, fields, and actions appear connected to the wrong content. Typography and color systems must support comprehension, hierarchy, states, and brand without sacrificing access. Readable line length, line height, font size, resilient system fallbacks, sufficient contrast, and non-color cues matter across zoom and rendering conditions. Brand colors are inputs to refine, not exemptions from contrast. Consistency means comparable controls and states follow predictable patterns, while different purposes remain distinguishable. Feedback should be timely, specific, visible, programmatically exposed, and proportional to the action. Hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error, empty, and success states need a shared behavioral language and clear recovery. Apply it to community survey interface design critique.
